item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have come to be a big fan of these Chinese mythology ones, partially because I don't really know much about Chinese mythology and they have inspired me to learn. I really like your ideas, they complement the mythos nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there an upper limit to volume of water converted? If not, dropping this thing in the ocean would soon* fuck up the whole planet. A good example of why Safe != safe.

*20 liters/second doesn't sound like it would put a dent in the ocean, but when you factor in the 2^n…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the anomalous effects and the second interpretation, even though I'm not that up to date with the Sun Wukong myths. I'm still a bit unsure about the chain stuff.

And I can't help but to imagine a writing system based on pictograms of baby seals when I read Small Seal Script.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Water with iron characteristics?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Straight forward story, even with the intended mystery written in- I enjoyed this a lot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although, before realizing that the entire lake might revert, step 5 might've taken a couple of weeks worth of drilling to be re-drilled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh hey, you posted this. Huzzah. I am not late to this party at all.

I like this article. Its brevity keeps it from dragging its feet, and your use of Chinese myth is just as interesting here as it was in 2481. Additionally, the well provides a good hook for me at the end.

+1. Good job Sunny. A solid SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say,
I HATE STORY WITH OPEN ENDING.
AND
I HATE STORY THAT HARD TO UNDERSTAND

but still +1. For No reason.

item.title: Come for the anomalous water, stay for the snake people!
item.content:encoded: I liked the mythology tie-in, and also the Safe designation. If you drop it in the ocean, you could precipitate an extinction level event; keep it in a waterproof box, it's harmless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like ice-nine. I like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would hope that Site doesn't have a "deluge"-type fire suppression system…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually sat down and figured out the whole process because I am a total nerd for engineering.

You'd want to do something like this.
Warning: critical nerdery levels detected.

Basically, this combines some tricks they use for drilling into ice in Antarctica, where water can re-freeze in seconds, others they use for drilling oil wells and tunnels into hard but unstable rock, and some standard pier construction methods.

This assumes they've figured out that water reverts when separated from the anomaly, and that it's probably related to the thing they can see a couple meters under the surface. From there, what you do is:

  1. Helicopter in a small heavy-duty drilling rig. (These do actually exist. They have mining, quarrying, and construction applications.)
  2. Mount rig on a flat barge and drag to just above the anomaly under the lake.
  3. Drill set of small bore holes through lake around anomaly; hammer in anchor piles.
    • this assumes "what happens to the rest of the lake when the anomaly is gone" has occurred to them
    • As with arctic drilling, the neat part is that the "melting" water acts as both slurry and coolant. Also like arctic drilling, a fast-cycle pump with a set of chambered valves quickly sucks out liquid water before it can "refreeze" and wreak havoc on your machinery.
  4. Drill a pilot hole to just above Sun Wukong, and line the whole thing with pipe as you go to keep your hole open.
  5. switch to large tunnel-boring bit. Using the first pipe as a guide, expand the hole and insert utility pipe sections. At the end, you have a roughly 2.25 meter hole lined with pipe above the anomaly, preferably big enough for a reseus macaque and a large chunk of iron-water to fit through. Cap this with a watertight seal. Last thing you need is a freak rainstorm refilling a week's worth of drilling.
  6. Drill a second hole like this, but at an angle landing it a safe distance underneath SW.
  7. Now comes the tricky part. Carefully drill a series of holes around SW, connecting the upper and lower tunnels.
  8. Ream out the area between these holes, and gradually build a complete watertight connection between the upper and lower tunnels, leaving only a thin link of Iron-water between the rest of the lake and the now isolated chunk around SW.
  9. Run a final check that your anchor columns are up to the job of supporting your construction project in normal water. You're starting over if you get this wrong.
  10. sever the connection between the chunk and the lake.
  11. Slam a plug into the hole to keep the water from flooding in faster than you can pull the now-free chunk out of the tunnel and onto the barge.
  12. Get it to shore, and as far from the water as you can.
  13. Proceed with archeology, discover it turns off when less than 20 liters of water are present.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 557 does pretty much the same thing too. Probably less like an origin story and more like another section of life for him. Another century, another paragovernmental organization imprisoning the supernatural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So is this the origin story of

Spoiler?
Hide spoiler

SCP-682?


;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The heavy mythological bent really makes this article. For a few commenters it seems to be the weakest part, but for me it is the other way around - it is the strongest and makes it stand on its own. The Xia snake people connection isn't even necessary, though it being there doesn't hurt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. Is produced by the glory of Broken God And maybe, but I'd say they'd be more worried about this thing being dropped into the sea or something :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to clarify that I'm not really just, importing what is already in the myth, into a SCP format. I used several concept in several a bit related myths yes, but the story in this is not part of the original myth. I aslo didn't really go with the original myth concept of what 金箍棒 is like.

But nevertheless, it's understable that you feel this way, and thanks for the feedback :3

item.title: Re: Solid but...
item.content:encoded: I was hoping that the piece could convey a story to the readers without the knowledge of the myth, and for those who know the myth, it's will add up to it. But my bad if I didn't do it that ideally :3 Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They don't really need to get only the needle out exactly, they could have just cut a cube with it out, and with less than 20 litres of -1 attached to the needle, -1 turns back to water, at which point they were able to get the needle out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2711 was found in the right paw of a non-anomalous rhesus macaque (Macaca mulatta), embedded 2.3 meters below the surface of a lake on Mount Tongbai, Henan Province, China.

Upon the removal of SCP-2711, SCP-2711-1 turned back into lake water.

How did they remove the iron-like-water from around the needle without damaging the needle itself?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really enjoy pre-packaged myth ported over into the SCP format. The whole conceit is always "what if this well-established mythologic concept was actually real", which I find to be fairly boring, especially because in these cases, the answer is always "well it would be locked away and no one would know about it."

Something about this kind of article has always felt to me like a rote exercise in adapting existing ideas to the main list format.

item.title: Solid but...
item.content:encoded:
You lost me at the ancient myths…

Which is a heavy personal bias. I personally dislike when really well written SCPs (which this is, still +1) are explained by ancient lore/history. Just my opinion, not affecting the rating just throwing this out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sweet. I always wanted to write an SCP about the 如意金箍棒, but I could never figure out how to put it down in words. So instead of actually growing or shrinking, it just transfers the property of iron to nearby water, right? Good JOb.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just looking for clarity here - the water has the properties of iron, while still looking like water (just water that's holding very, very still)?

If so, the Foundation would be extremely interested in figuring out how the anomalous effect is produced. The potential benefits of being able to create transparent iron items seem obvious…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You misspelled iron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well Needles that can turn Water into Ice.
Also connections with ancient myths.
Verdict +1 Always.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A short piece related to some Chinese myth. Great thanks to Athena_Grey, Metaphysician, Lex1nat0r, Jekeled, TwistedGears, Soulless, minmin, MrWrong, Ireul, ARD, Faminepulse and all the others who reviewed the skip.

+ Related Myths
- Related Myths
  • Yu the Great/大禹, the first king of Xia Dynasty, once dealt with great floods.
  • Sun Wukong/孙悟空's weapon, Ruyi Jingu Bang/如意金箍棒, was originally used by Yu during his flood control, and was called 定海神针.
  • Yu the Great fought a water monster during his flood control, who he defeated and locked under a well. (In some versions of the myth, the well is in Mount Tongbai) One version goes that the monster is ape-like, called 巫支祁, who said to be one of the prototypes Sun Wukong based on. The other version goes that the monster is a Jiaolong (an aquatic dragon, but less powerful and divine). [禹王锁蛟]
+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

There are (at least) two different interpretations of what happened, based on which version of the myth it uses. Other interpretations are aslo possible, as the piece is intended to be open-ended.

First version:
Sun Wukong was defeated by Yu and locked in a well, he escaped after the chains become weak. He stole Yu's needle because he hated him, and crossed out the original text, engraving his own. Years later, he converted to Buddism (after maybe going through what happened in Journey to the West), and realized his wrong doings. He returned to where he was imprisoned, and passed away there. He could have simply died, or he could have abandoned his mortal body and ascended to godhood (became a Buddha).

Second version:
Sun Wukong stumbled upon Yu's needle by chance and claimed it his own. As he didn't know better and was arrogant, he crossed out the original text, engraving his own. Years later, he converted to Buddism (after maybe going through what happened in Journey to the West), and began to do good. At that time, the dragon/snake monster, who was imprisoned by Yu was about to break out, feeling that he owed Yu a great debt, he fought the dragon and in the end, used the needle to covert the water of the lake, imprision the dragon again. The mudslide was cause not by the lake's conversion back into water, but by the dragon escaping.

Minor Points that did not make into the piece, but are what I had in mind:

  • Fuxi is what Xia Dynasty snake people call the Broken God. The needle is a Broken God relic, remodified by Yu the Great to control floods. (Putting it into the rivers will obviously immediately stop the flood :P) It might originally have other anomalous properties.
  • The Xia Dynasty snake people don't see the Broken God/Mekhane/Fuxi as broken — they think it used itself to trap Yaldabaoth. Thus they see Broken God relics as the tools Broken God used or things manifested by its mind.
  • The well was spared from the massive reality wipe the Xia Dynasty went through, is because the chains originally had reality stablizing ability — it is composed of the same material with SCP-2481-2. But they later lost that property because of the long duration of time.
